title: Read counts
text:
MaAsLin3 identifies prevalence (presence/absence) associations. Prevalence of organisms can be greatly affected by the read depth of the different samples. When using abundance values (not quantitative abundance values) the read numbers/depth for each sample should be included as a continuous fixed effect.


We have the read counts/depth in two separate filesfor this dataset. Read these in and check their contents.

#Read in the export files
export_40 <- read.csv("export_samples_2026-04-16_14_40.tsv", sep = "\t")
export_41 <- read.csv("export_samples_2026-04-16_14_41.tsv", sep = "\t")
#Check
export_40 |> dplyr::slice(1:5)
export_41 |> dplyr::slice(1:5)

Thankfully the sample names in the Name variable are the same as in our count table and metadata. Unfortunately, the read counts are set as characters with M, and G representing 1,000,000s and 1,000,000,000s.



Let’s check if there are any other letters used. In the code we use the stringr::sub_str() function.

#Check letters used in read counts
export_40 |>
    #Pull Reads out as a vector
    dplyr::pull(Reads) |>
    #Extract last character
    stringr::str_sub(-1) |>
    #See unique values
    unique()
#Repeat for export_41
export_41 |>
    dplyr::pull(Reads) |>
    stringr::str_sub(-1) |>
    unique()

We can see the letters k, M, and G are used. There are some numbers but these are from read counts with less than 1,000 reads.
In your future analysis I would recommend looking at the entire table to see if it looks all ok before continuing. Of course, if you can get the actual whole numbers of reads that would be ideal (i.e. 3,543,297 rather than 3.543M).



In the next code block we’ll bind these two data.frames, and select the Names and Reads columns. Then we’ll need to change the Reads column to be proper numeric values. We’ll carry this out with the following steps:

Mutate the character to change k to e3, M to e6 etc.

e3 is scientific notation meaning times 10 to the power of 3 (i.e. *1000)

Convert the Reads to numerics

e3 will be converted to a trailing 000, e6 to 000000 etc.
167e3 -> 167000



#Edits
read_counts <- export_40 |>
    #Bind the 2 data.frames by rows (one on top of the other)
    dplyr::bind_rows(export_41) |>
    #Keep only Name and Reads columns
    dplyr::select(Name, Reads) |>
    #Change letters to R exponential notation
    #e.g. 10k becomes 10e3
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("k","e3",Reads)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("K","e3",Reads)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("M","e6",Reads)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("G","e9",Reads)) |>
    #Convert read numbers to numerics
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=as.numeric(Reads))
#Check
read_counts |> slice(1:5)

Great, that looks all good.



Our final step is to bind the read counts to the metadata table correctly.
First, let’s extract the sample names from the metadata table.

#Extract row names (sample names) from metadata
samples <- row.names(metadata)

With this vector we can remove any samples in the read counts table that are not present in the metadata.

#Remove samples in read_counts not present in metadata
read_counts <- read_counts |>
    dplyr::filter(Name %in% samples)

Next we’ll ensure the order of samples in read_counts is the same as in metadata.

#Match the order of the 2 dfs by the sample names
read_counts <- read_counts[order(match(read_counts$Name, samples)),]
#Check read_counts Name column is identical to row names of metadata
identical(row.names(metadata), read_counts$Name)

If the TRUE statement appears from the identical() function you can add the read counts to metadata. If FALSE appears you will need to do some debugging and fix the issue.

#Add read count column to metadata df
metadata$Reads <- read_counts$Reads
#Check
metadata[1:5,]

With our metadata finalised we can move onto the MaAsLin3 analysis.

title: Plots
text:
Plots
Many plots of significant associations are created by maaslin3() . The types of plots can be grouped into 3 main types:

The summary plot

This shows abundance and prevalence modelling information for the top 25 features

Linear plots

These are for the abundance based linear modelling
Box plots are produced for categorical fixed effects
Scatter plots with a linear model line are produced for continuous fixed effects

Logistics plots

These are for the prevalence based logistic modelling
Grids are produced for categorical fixed effects
Box plots are produced for continuous fixed effects


In your own analysis you would start by looking at the summary plot. However, we will look at the other plots beforehand to get a better understanding of the coefficient values so we can better interpret the summary plot.
But before that we will recreate the plots in the next page.

title: Abundance
text:
In this main section of this course you’ll be introduced to MaAsLin3 analysis using the “abundance” dataset.


The dataset consists of 172 shotgun metagenomics samples classified to bacterial species. The metadata includes:

SampleSite: Axilla, Cheek, or Scalp
TimepointName: One of two sets of pool visits

Poolvisit1,2,3
Poolvisit7,8,9


We also have the read depth for each sample which we will incorporate intot he metadata and analysis.



The steps we will carry out are:

Data importing & formatting

Importing & formatting the count table
Importing the metadata
Importing & formatting the read depths followed by adding it to the metadata

MaAslin3 analysis

Description of the lme4 formula
Explanation and usage of fixed effects
Explanation and usgae of random effects
Median comparisons and their considerations

Text output

Converting significant results text table to an interactive html table
Metric defintions

Plot outputs & coefficients

Recreating plots
Boxplots for categorical linear/abundance associations
Scatter plot for continuous linear/abundance associations
Tile plots for categorical logistics/prevalence associations
Boxplots for continuous logistics/prevalence associations
Summary plot with coefficient plot and heatmap of top 25 features

title: Notebook setup
text:
Notebook setup
The first step is to ensure you have carried out the setup instructions and run through the jupyter tutorial if you are not familiar with it.
Prior to analysis you’ll need to create a new jupyter notebook called metagenome_analysis in the metagenome directory.
At the top create a markdown cell with the following headers.

# Metagenome
## Libraries

Then we’ll load all the libraries we need in a new code cell.

library("maaslin3")
library("tidyverse")
library("IRdisplay")
library("DT")

Now we’re ready to start with data importing and processing.

title: Plot types
text:
As a recap see the four different types of plots below.


For abundance associations.


Box plot showing the log base 2 relative abundance values of the features.




Coefficient represents the fold change from the reference to the query group.



Scatter plot of log base 2 relative abundance (y) against continuous effect (x, read depth in below figure) with best fit line.




Coefficient represents the amount of change the variable causes, i.e. the slope.




For prevalence associations.


Tile plot showing the number of samples where the feature is present and absent for each grouping.




The coefficient indicates the difference of log-odds/logit of the feature being present in query samples compared to the reference samples.



Box plot of the continuous value (read depth in below figure) split by samples where the feature is present and absent.




The coefficient represents the amount of increased/decreased likelihood (log-odds/logit) of the feature’s presence with a higher value of the metadata.

title: Fixed effects
text:
Fixed effects are any term related to your hypothesis that requires evaluation and any continuous control variables.
We are interested in the differences of our three body sites (Axilla, Cheek and Scalp) so we are using it as a fixed effect. We are also interested in the difference from the first set of pool visits to the next set so we used TimepointName as a fixed effect.
To control for the read counts/depths of the samples we will use Reads as a fixed effect. This uses it as a continuous control variable.


When using fixed effects you can look at the main effect, interactions, or both of multiple variables.
The main effects is when looking at the effect of one variable by itself. Iteractions and when you combine variables.
In our formula we specified the formula to only look at main effects. We will look at iteractions later.



Below is a list of examples on how to specify the usage of fixed effects.

a: Main effect of a
a + b: Main effect of a and b (no iteraction)
a:b: Interaction of a and b (no main effects)
a * b: Main effects and interaction of a and b (expands to a + b + a:b)
(a+b+c)^2: Main effects and 2-way interactions (no 3-way interaction) of a, b , and c (expands to a + b + c + a:b + a:c + b:c)
(a+b)*c: all main effects and pairwise interactions between a and c, and b and c (not between a and b) (expands to a + b + c + a:c + b:c)
0 + a: 0 suppresses the intercept resulting in a model that has one parameter per level of a (identical to : a - 1)

title: Metadata
text:
As with most analysis we require metadata. Our metadata table requires:

Columns of the different metadata variables

The column names are the names of the variables

Rows of observations per sample

The row names need to contain the same sample names as the count table




The first step is to read in and check our metadata.

#Read in metadata
metadata <- read.csv("Metagenome_metadata.csv", sep=",", row.names=1)
#Check rows 1 to 5
metadata |> dplyr::slice(1:5)




There are three main types of metadata variables for MaAsLin3. These are:

Categorical fixed effects

A categorical variable that is related to your hypothesis and requires evaluation
These need to be factors in your metadata with the first level as the reference group
All other levels are compared to the reference in pairwise fashion
For example healthy vs disease states
We will use SampleSite and TimePointName as categorical fixed effects

Continuous fixed effects

This could be a continuous variable that is related to your hypothesis and requires evaluation (e.g. dosage)
Or it could be a continuous control variable (e.g. age)
We will use Reads (read counts/depth) as a continuous control variable (see the (read counts page)[/abundance/read_counts.qmd])

Random effects

Categorical variables to include that you are not necessarily interested in evaluating
These don’t need to be factors since a reference group is not required
An example includes experimental blocks, different sequencing runs, or subjects
We will use SubjectID as a random effect


More theory and considerations are included in the later pages:

Fixed effects
Random effects




We are going to use SampleSite and TimePointName as categorical fixed effects. Therefore, we need to convert them to factors.
Before factorisation, let’s see what groupings are in the two variables.

metadata |> dplyr::pull(SampleSite) |> unique()
metadata |> dplyr::pull(TimepointName) |> unique()

We have 3 for SampleSite. Let’s use Axilla as the reference group.
We only have 2 for TimePointName and we will use Pool_visits1,2,3 as the reference group since its the first set of visits.
Note: The group/s compared to the reference group is/are called the query group/s.
Type and run the below code to factorise the two metadata variables. The code uses some piping and the dplyr::mutate() function.

#Factorise categorical fixed effects
metadata <- metadata |>
    #Factorise SampleSite with Axilla as the reference level
    dplyr::mutate(SampleSite=factor(SampleSite, 
                    levels = c("Axilla", "Cheek", "Scalp"))) |>
    #Factorise TimepointName with the first set of visits as the reference level
    dplyr::mutate(TimepointName=factor(TimepointName, 
                    levels = c("Pool_visits1,2,3","Pool_visits7,8,9")))

Great, next we’ll add read counts/depth to the metadata table.

title: Continuous logistic plots
text:
Box plots are used to visualise prevalence/logistic modelling associations with continuous effects. These show the range of read sizes for samples where the feature is absent and samples where the feature is absent.
We will use the Reads grouping to look at continuous logistic results.
In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only prevalence model and read rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display box plots



Create a significant result tibble only containing Reads associations with the prevalence model.

#Create a tbl with our sig results of interest
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata Reads rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "Reads") |>
    #Filter to retain prevalence model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "prevalence")
#Glimpse the tibble
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |> dplyr::glimpse()

Extract the top 5 associations by their q-values (qval_indiviudal).

#Top 5 significant associations for prevalence modelling of Reads
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_indivudal values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12,14,15))

In this case we actually get 6 rows. This is because the q-value of the 5th and 6th row are identical (i.e. they are both 5th equal place).
The results aren’t terribly significant with fairly low coefficients (<2).



Let’s look at the top association:

Feature: Malassezia globosa
Coefficient: 1.300098
Q-value: 0.002123845

For continuous metadata the coefficient represents the amount of increased/decreased likelihood of the feature’s presence with a higher value of the metadata. A positive value for Reads would indicate the feature is more likely to be present with samples of higher read counts. In other words a positive coefficient means the logit of the feature being present increases.



Logistic continuous plots are horizontal box plots of read counts seperated into 2 groupings:

Samples where the feature is absent
Samples where the feature is present

Logistic categorical plots shows us how many samples the feature is present and absent in for the different groupings. Let’s look at an example.


Below is the box plot created for the logistic modeling of Malassezia globosa for the reads effect.




In this case the plot shows:

Samples where the feature are present tend to have higher read counts
There are more samples where the feature is present (N (not zero) = 145) compared to where it is absent (172-145=27).





Look at the most significant results of associations with negative coefficients.

#Top 5 significant associations with negative coefficients
# for prevalence modelling of Reads
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_indivudal values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,11,12,14,15))

There only is one association which has the error: “Prevalence association possibly induced by stronger abundance association”. Additionally, its individual q-value is >0.1 meaning there will not be a plot of it. It was included in the significant reuslts table as its joint q-value is < 0.1.
In fact the association is of our old friend Pseudomonas_E lactis. Its linear prevalence association was very strong for the continuous linear analysis and plots. Therefore, its very likely the above error is true and the prevalence association is due to the abudnance assoication.
Logically it makes sense that you are more likely to get positive coefficient values as the more reads you have the more likely you are to detect all organisms present.



Attempt the below MCQs based on the prevalence modelling with the Reads fixed effect:

Which feature association has the highest coefficient value?

 *Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris Cutibacterium granulosum Staphylococcus haemolyticus


Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E

Which feature has the highest amount of samples where the feature is absent?

 *Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris Cutibacterium granulosum Staphylococcus haemolyticus


Open and view the reads logistic plots for the following features to answer the MCQs.

Malassezia globosa
Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E
Staphylococcus capitis
Xanthomonas campestris

Tip: The reads/x-axis scale is identical for all the plots making for easier comparisons.

Which feature has the smallest difference ion the median values for the absent and present groupings?

 *Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris



Which feature has the highest amount of outliers for the Present box (i.e. points outside the range of the box and whiskers)?

 *Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris



Which feature has the highest median value for the absent samples?

 *Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris


Using the object sig_res_reads_prev_tbl, create some code to answer the below questions.

How many associations have a qval_indivudal value of less than 0.1?

 12 26 172



How many associations are there where the feature is absent in more than 50% of the samples?

 12 26 172


Tip: You can carry out mathematical operations on different columns in a dplyr::filter() function and carry out a comparison/filter on its result.


Code solution


#MCQs
## Significant results with individual q-value < 0.1
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
   filter(qval_individual<0.1) |>
   nrow()
## Number of results where the feature is absent in more than
# 50% of the samples
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
   filter(N_not_zero/N < 0.5) |>
   nrow()

title: Categorical linear boxplots
text:
Boxplots are used to visualise abundance/linear modelling associations with categorical fixed effects. To learn about these we will look at Sample site association to interpret the metrics and plots. As a reminder the Sample site comparisons were:

Cheek (query) against Axilla (reference)
Scalp (query) against Axilla (reference)

In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only abundance model and SampleSite rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display box plots



To start we’ll create a significant result tibble only containing SampleSite associations with the abundance model.

#Tibble of significant abundance associations for SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata SampleSite rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "SampleSite") |>
    #Filter to retain abundance model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "abundance")
#Glimpse the tibble
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl |> dplyr::glimpse()

With this tibble we’ll extract the top 5 associations by their q values (qval_indiviudal).

#Top 5 lowest qval individual values species by abundance for SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl |> 
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_indivudal values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12))

You’ll see the top association is:

feature: JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290
name: SampleSiteScalp
coefficient: 5.639159

But what does the coefficient mean?



The coefficient value of abundance modelling with categorical data represents the fold change in relative abundance from the reference group to the query group. MaAsLin’s manual’s description is: “a one-unit change in the metadatum variable corresponds to a 2coef fold change in the relative abundance of the feature.
In our data the coefficient of JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290 for Scalp is 5.639159. This indicates the relative abundance of the feature is >32 times larger in scalp (query) samples compared to axilla (reference) samples.
In the above we say >32 times as 25 is 32. The actual value of 2^5.639159 is 49.83747.

You can open it in the jupyter file explorer. Its path is ./metagenome_output/figures/association_plots/SampleSite/linear/SampleSite_JANJXX01.sp..hMGS.05290_linear.png. The breakdown of this path is:

metagenome_output/: The output directory specified and created by maaslin3()
figures/: The directory with all output figures

Each subdiretcory is for each specified fixed effect (i.e. Reads, SampleSite, and TimepointName)

    width = 600)

The features of the plot are:

Shows the number of samples for each category (e.g. Axilla has 34 samples)

With this plot we can clearly see that the relative abundance of the scalp samples is much higher than for Axilla. We can see that the median value for Scalp is around -5 (relative abundance = ~0.005) whereas its around -12 (relative abundance = ~ 0.00001) for Axilla.
It also looks like Cheek vs Axilla might be a significant associations. Open your significant_results.html and search for “JANJXX01”. You’ll see that the association for Cheek is present, and therefore significant, with a coefficient of 4.05 and a q value of 2.34e-7.



Please attempt the following tasks:


View the metrics of the top five significant SampleSite associations with negative coefficient values. I.e. filter out associations with positive values and then look at the five associations with the lowest q values

Which feature association has the highest significance (i.e lowest q-value)?

 Staphylococcus epidermidis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteCheek Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus hominis-SampleSiteScalp Cutibacterium avidum-SampleSite Cheek



Which feature association has the greatest coefficient value (i.e. the value furtherest from 0 representing the largest fold change difference comparing the query to the reference)?

 Staphylococcus epidermidis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteCheek Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus hominis-SampleSiteScalp Cutibacterium avidum-SampleSite Cheek



Which feature association has the highest prevalence (i.e. the largest N_not_zero value)?

 Staphylococcus epidermidis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteCheek Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus hominis-SampleSiteScalp Cutibacterium avidum-SampleSite Cheek




Code solution


#View the metrics of the top five significant associations with negative coefficients
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl |> 
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5)





Using the relevant plots answer the below MCQs:

Approximately, what is the median log relative abundance of Staphylococcus epidermidis for the Axilla samples?

 -2.5 -12.5 -15.0



Approximately, what is the median log relative abundance of Staphylococcus hominis for the Scalp samples?

 -2.5 -12.5 -15.0



Approximately, what is the median log relative abundance of Staphylococcus lugdunensis for the Scalp samples?

 -2.5 -12.5 -15.0






Important to note that the abundance association tests use samples where the feature is present. This means associations where the feature is more prevalent will have greater power.
This can be seen with our Staphylococcus lugdunensis associations. The fold change is greater for the Scalp against Axilla association (-8.56) compared to the Cheek against Axilla association (-5.14). However, the q value of the Cheek association is more significant (6e-11 compared to 1.6-e09). This is because there are 26 Cheek samples with S.lugdunensis present compared to only 11 for the Scalp samples.

title: Random effects
text:
A random effect is a categorical control variable to include that you are not necessarily interested in evaluating but what to control for. These include:

Experimental blocks
Common spatial groupings that create non-independence (e.g. samples from the same people or country)
Temporal groupings that also create non-independence (e.g. samples collected from different places in the same year)

In our analysis we are using the SubjectID variable as a random effect.


Random effects are always included by writing elements in () with a |. On the left of the | you specify the random effect/s. On the right of the | you specify the grouping factor/s on which the random effects depend on, these need to be of class factor.
Examples include:

(1|s): Random intercepts for unique level of factor s
(1|s) + (1|i): Random intercept for each unique level of s and each unique level of i
(1|s/i): Random intercepts for factor s and i, where the random effects of i are nested in s.

Exapnds to (1|s) + (1|s:i), i.e. a random intercept for each level of s, and each unique combination of the levels s and i.
Nested random effects are used in so-called multilevel models, For example, s might refer to schools/forest, and i to classroom/tree within the school/forest.

(a|s): Random intercept and random slopes for a, for each level of s

Correlations between the intercept and slope effects are also estimated

(a*b|s): Random intercepts and slopes for a, b, and the a:b interaction, for each level of s.

Correlation between all the random effects are estimated

(0+a|s): Random slopes for a for each level of s, but no random intercepts
(a||s): Random intercepts and random slopes for a, for each level of s, but no correlations between the random effect (i.e. they are set to 0)

Expands to (0+a|s) + (1|s)


You are most likely to only use grouping factors. In our formula are random effect specification is (1|SubjectID) so we are using SubjectID as a grouping effect.



Alternatives to random effects
Random effects are not always the solution to every correlated data problem. Logistic random intercept models may produce poor model fits when there are fewer than 4 average observations per random effect group (e.g. less than four samples come form participant 1).
Recommendations:

>= 4 average observations per group

e.g. subject sampled >= 4 times
Use random effect with small_random_effects=FALSE (default)
Random effects are robust and increase power

< 4 average observations per group, metadata not collinear with the groups

e.g. Subjects sampled once before and once after treatment
Use random effects for abundance but fixed effect for prevalence by setting small_random_effect=TRUE
Prevalence random effects are not robust but fixed effects are robust and control for grouping

< 4 average observations per group, metadata collinear with the groups

Subjects have fixed disease status and are sampled twice (disease status is constant per-subject, so collinear with the grouping)
Subset or average data to one observation per group


Therefore, it is always good to check the number of observations per group of your random effects. The below code uses the functions dplyr::count() to show this for our SubjectID variable.

#Number of observations for each SubjectID grouping
metadata |>
    dplyr::count(SubjectID)

In this case all our observations have >=4 so our average will also be >= 4. Therefore using small_random_effects=FALSE in our maaslin3() analysis was the correct choice. Of course you should check this before running maaslin3() in your own analysis.
If it is not easy to determine whether the average is >=4 by viewing them all you can quickly calculate the average with the below code.

#Average number of observations per group
metadata |>
    dplyr::count(SubjectID) |>
    dplyr::pull(SubjectID) |> mean()

title: Replot
text:
When we ran maaslin3() it created 250 plot files as we set max_pngs = 250. Unfortunately this means that some of the significant associations we want to interrogate do not have plots.
Fortunately rather than rerunning the maaslin3() function, which can take a long time, we can run the function maaslin_plot_results_from_output().
Run the below code to recreate the plots whilst making more by setting max_pngs= to 500.

maaslin3::maaslin_plot_results_from_output(
    output = 'metagenome_output',
    metadata = metadata,
    normalization = 'TSS',
    transform = 'LOG',
    #Max value of qval_individual value for plot to be generated
    #qval_joint has no effect on this
    max_significance = 0.1,
    median_comparison_abundance = TRUE,
    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
    max_pngs = 500
)



Unfortunately when running maaslin_plot_results_from_output() we can’t just copy the parameters from our maaslin3() function. But at the same time maaslin_plot_results_from_output() uses some of the same parameters as maaslin3() and we need to have these being identical to our previous maaslin3() function.
Below our the parameters we used:

output: This is the output directory of our maaslin3() function

Our replot function will uses its contents to recreate the plots
The recreated and new plots will be placed in the relevant directories within this directory

metadata: The metadata table to be used for the plot generation

We are using the same table as our maaslin3() function
This can be an opportunity to rename variables and values (see: MaAsLin3 manual on replotting)

normalization: Same as maaslin3()
transform: Same as maaslin3()
max_significance = 0.1: Same as maaslin3()

This indicates the qval_individual value of the features

median_comparison_abundance: Same as maaslin3()
median_comparison_prevalence: Same as maaslin3()
max_pngs: New maximum number of pngs to create

If there are less significant features than the max number set you will get as many plots as significant features

title: Mamba
text: Mamba

title: Mixed effect model
text:
In real life we will commonly have more than two variables in our experiments. Thankfully, linear regression can have three or more variables.
Once you start using more variables they can start to interact with each other in various ways. This is when Mixed Effect Modelling, also known as Hierarchical modelling, can be useful. This is important for us to know as MaAsLin3 stands for Microbiome Multivariable Associations with Linear Models and uses Mixed effect linear models.
In simplest term a mixed effect model is when you have multiple linear regressions for your data based on different nested groups. To explain this further we’ll go through the following concepts:

Nested data
Random intercept
Random slope
Random slope + intercept



Nested data is when your observation are within groups of data. This could be:

Age groups
Demographics
Gender
Experimental blocks
Sequencing runs

Let’s look at a concrete example with our E.coli data from the last page. However, in this case we have more datapoints. Our original data was 75 patients with E.coli that had no detected AMRs. Our new set of 75 patients had E.coli with AMRs. These two sets of patients are our two nested groups.
Note: You can have a lot more than just 2 nested groups.









In this case we can see that our simple linear model based only on E.coli abundance and Antibiotic dosage is not fit for purpose.



The first addition to our linear model to incorporate mixed effects is to add random intercepts. In essence this is allowing each group to have different intercept values.
Let’s see that in practice with our dataset.









The linear models for each nested group now start in good places since they have different intercepts. However, they both have the same slope which does not fit either group very well.



Instead of random intercepts lets use random slopes. This gives each group its own slope.
Below is our data with random slopes but the same intercept.









This looks better but its still not perfect.



In mixed effect models both random slopes and random intercepts are used. This allows you to best account for the differences between the groups.
Below is our final plot with random slopes and random intercepts.









Nice explanation: https://mfviz.com/hierarchical-models/

title: Linear model
text:
MaAsLin3 carries out linear modelling for abundance testing.


A simple linear regression can be carried two variables. These variables are:

The independent variable: Not dependent on any other variable

Preferably controlled by the experimenter

The dependant variable: Dependant on the independent variable

The below linear regression equation then allows you to predict the value of y when you have the value of x or vice verse.
\[
y = mx + c
\]
Where:

\(y\) = Y variable (dependant variable)
\(m\) = slope coefficient
\(x\) = X variable (independent variable)
\(c\) = intercept coefficient (i.e. y position when x = 0)

title: Jupyter
text:
Jupyter-notebook is a nice browser based method to write, edit, and run code. It was initally created for Python coding, but has since branched out to many other languages, such as R.
We are using it in this workshop for a variety of its properties:

It is popular and well maintained.
It is lightweight. Other heavier weight programs, such as RStudio, would struggle in our HPC due to the graphical and CPU load.
It is interactive and displays code output.
It allows for easier annotation, editing, and debugging than the command line.
It provides a graphical interface for changing directories and choosing files.

Before carrying out any analysis we will go through a quick tutorial of jupyter-notebook.
Note: There is a video tutorial of this chapter at the bottom of this page if you prefer to watch it.


The first step is to open jupyter-notebook. Run the below command in your (r_community) environment.

jupyter-notebook

This will open jupyter-notebook in firefox. We won’t need to access the linux terminal anymore. Leave the terminal running jupyter-notebook and full screen your firefox so you should see something like below.


Can’t see the whole webVNC window?

You may need to zoom out with your browser so you can see the full webVNC window.

Chrome: Click on the three dots in vertical line (



      


) on the top left for a dropdown menu which includes zoom options. - Edge: Click on the three horizontal lines (


      


) on the top left for a dropdown menu which includes zoom options. - Firefox: Click on the three dots in horizontal line (


  


) on the top left for a dropdown menu which includes zoom options.







The next step is to create an R notebook.

Click on the “New” button towards the top right, right of the “Upload” button.
From the dropdown click “R” below “Python 3 (ipykernel)”.

This will open up a new R notebook like below.






Jupyter-notebook uses cells (the grey boxes) to separate code. This is very useful to compartmentalise our code.
There will already be one cell. Within the cell, type in the below commands.

1+1
2-3

When pressing enter in cells it will create a new line. To run all commands in a cell press CTRL + enter.
Run your current cell and you should see something like below.






You can create new cells by 2 different means.

Press the + button on the tool bar (between the floppy disk



  


and scissors


      


). This will add a cell below your currently selected cell. - Click on the Insert button and use the dropdown to add a cell above or below your currently selected cell.
Tip: Hover over the toolbar icons to display a text based description of its function.
With that knowledge add a second cell below the first cell. Add the following code to your second cell but do not run it.

num_1 <- 3
num_2 <- 10

Tip: Notice there are green lines around your selected cell.
Insert a third cell and add the following code to it. Do not run the code.

num_1 * num_2




Try to run the code in the third cell. There should be an error as we have not created the objects num_1 & num_2. We have only written the code for these objects but not run them.
We can run all the code in a notebook starting from the first cell to the last cell.
To run all cells from the start:

Click on the “Cell” button.
Click “Run All” from the drop-down options.

You should then see something like the below in your notebook.



There is no output printed for cell 2 because we are assigning variables. However, the correct output for Cell 3 is below it. This is because the variables were assigned in cell 2 before cell 3 was run.






As with RStudio and other good coding interfaces we can save our notebook.
First we should rename the file. Rename the notebook to “jupyter_tut.ipynb”:

Click on the name of the notebook, currently called “Untitled”.

This is at the very top of the notebook, right of the Jupyter logo.

A pop-up called “Rename Notebook” will appear. Change the Name to “jupyter_tut.ipynb”.
Click “Rename”.

Now we can save the file. Two methods to save are:

Click the floppy disk



  


on the toolbar. - Click on the “File” button. Click “Save and Checkpoint” from the dropdown options.



We will be using multiple notebooks in this workshop. We will also have multiple sections per notebook. It will be useful to create header cells with markdown to create visual separation of the different sections.
To add a header cell to the top of our notebook:

Create a new cell at the top of the notebook.
Click on the “Code” drop down and select “Markdown”.

The “Heading” option no longer works.






Add the following to the “Markdown” cell to create a first level header.

Ensure you have a space between the # and header text (“Tutorial”).



# Tutorial

Great, we can now add nice headers in our notebooks. Save the notebook once more before carrying on to the next section.


Markdown

You won’t need to know more about Markdown but if you are interested please see the Markdown guide.




To close the notebook:

Click on “File”.
From the dropdown options click “Close and Halt”.

When you are back in the file explorer page you may not yet see the new file you saved. If so, you will need to refresh the page with the Refresh button


    


towards the top right.



With that quick tutorial of jupyter-notebook we can start our community analysis in the next chapter.
For more info on jupter-notebook please see the appendix.

title: Background
text:
Background
General info on maaslin3. Talk about abundance versus prevalence testing.
Good resource: https://mspeekenbrink.github.io/sdam-r-companion/linear-mixed-effects-models.html#formulating-and-estimating-linear-mixed-effects-models-with-lme4

title: Logistic model
text:
MaAsLin3 carries out binary logistic regression for prevalence (absence/presence) testing.


Binary logistic regression is used to determine the probability of one of two outcomes. In MaAsLin3 this is to test prevalence, determining the probability of a feature to be present given an effect.
With this test we could attempt to answer the following example questions:

Is Helicobacter pylori more likely to be present in patients with ulcers compared to healthy controls?
Are Corynebacterium species more likely to be present in the Axilla compared to the cheek or scalp?
Are certain species more likely to be present/detected in samples with higher sampling depth?

We won’t go into the workings of binary logistics rather we’ll explain its main value: log-odds/logit.



Log-odds, also know as the logit, is equal to the logarithm of the odds. The equation is:
\[
logit = ln(p/1-p)
\]
where:

\(p\) = Probability
\(ln\) = log



For example, lets look at the probability of having the surname Murphy if you lived in Ireland in 2014.

Probability = 0.0164 (i.e 1.64%)
Odds = 0.1752
Logit = -4.044334

For a comparison, lets look at the probability of having the surname Gemmell if you lived in Scotland in 2014.

Probability = 0.0000446 (i.e 0.00446%)
Odds = 0.0000446
Logit = -10.01773

In the above case we can see that the logit has a lower negative value for Gemmell which had a lower probability than Murphy. BUt what is the scale of these values?



The logit scale goes from negative infinity (probability = 0) to positive infinity (probability = 1). Our anchor point is a probability of 50 with a logit value of 0.
Therefroe, a positive logit value means the event is more likely than not to occur (>0.5 probability). Whilst, a negative logit value means the event is more likely to not occur (<0.5 probability).
Some useful benchmark figures are:

Logit

-1.098612

-4.59512


0
-Infinite



Lets see the equation in the form of a plot:









In the plot we can see the logit value slowly changes moving away from a probability of 0.5. Once the probability is >0.99/<0.01 the logit value quickly increases/decreases getting closer and closer to infinity. This is the main reason we use logit scale. It is hard for us to see the difference between a probability of 0.000859 and 0.00006041 but easier for us to differentiate between their logit values of -7.058882 and -9.71429.
I hope this gives you a clear idea of the log-odds/logit value so you interrupt them in future analysis.
More information on how to interpret the logit value is found later in the abundance section: MaAsLin3 coefficients

title: Coefficients
text:
To explain the coefficient value we’ll go through two examples.


The feature in the first row is:

feature: JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290
name: SampleSiteScalp
coefficient: 5.63915856893338
model: abundance

This indicates that the relative abundance of JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290 is >32 times larger in the Scalp (query) samples compared to the Axilla (reference) samples.
In the above we say >32 times as 2^5 (R notation for “to the power of” is ^) is 32. The actual value of 2^5.63915856893338 is 49.83746.
Below is a tibble of 2 to the power of:


# A tibble: 1 × 11
  `2^0` `2^1` `2^2` `2^3` `2^4` `2^5` `2^6` `2^7` `2^8` `2^9` `2^10`
  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
1     1     2     4     8    16    32    64   128   256   512   1024


If the value is negative it indicates the relative abundance of the feature is 2^coef times less in the query compared to the reference.
For continuous metadata the coefficient represents the amount of change the variable causes, i.e. the slope. For example, a high positive value would represent a steep positive slope in a linear model. If we had this for our reads metadata that would indicate the abundance of the feature is likely to be higher if the sample has a higher read count compared to the samples with lower read counts. As maaslin3() analysed relative abundance values (normalization = 'TSS') we would ideally like no features to have significant associations with Reads.
This and the below points will hopefully be clearer after looking at the plots in the plots section.





The feature in the 20th row is:

feature: Streptococcus infantis_H
name: SampleSiteCheek
coefficient: 3.23027937141539
prevalence: prevalence

This indicates that the log-odds/logit of Streptococcus infantis_H being present in Cheek samples compared to Axilla samples is higher by 3.23. In other words the probability of Streptococcus infantis_H being present is higher in Cheek compared to Axilla. This could work if the log-odds of Cheek and Axilla were both negative, both positive, or if Cheek’s log-odds is positive whilst Axilla’s is negative. To know this we need to look at the plot.
As a sneak peak we will look at the plot for Streptococcus infantis_H. This feature is present in 49 out of the 60 cheek samples (82%) whilst only present 7 out of the 52 axilla samples (14%). This would be an instance of a positive log-odds for cheek and a negative one for Axilla.




On the other we still have a positive coefficient for cheek in the feature of the below plot (coef = 3.5). However, the log-odds/logit for Axilla and Cheek would both be negative, its just that Cheek’s log-odds is closer to 1 than Axilla.




If the value is negative it indicates the feature is less likely to be present in query samples compared to reference samples. The further the value is from 0 the stronger the association.



For continuous metadata the coefficient represents the amount of increased/decreased likelihood of the feature’s presence with a higher value of the metadata. A positive value for Reads would indicate the feature is more likely to be present with samples of higher read counts.
Finally, lets look at the plots which will help us interpret these coefficient values.

title: lme4 formula
text:
When using the formula= parameter of the maaslin3 function the formula needs to be specified in lme4 specification.
The following terms can be included in a lme4 formula:

Fixed effects
Random effects
Iteration terms
Others

The lme4 specification was originally created for the lme4 R package which stands for “Liner Mixed Effect Models using ‘Eigen’ and S4”.


The formula we specify to maaslin3() is a string that contains our formula. The string contains the column names from metadata that we want to use.
Below is the formula that we specified:
"~ SampleSite + TimepointName + Reads + (1|SubjectID)"


The first feature of note is the ~ operator. This separates the responses and the fixed and random effects



To the right of the ~ operator are the fixed effects and random effects.
Fixed effects are specified as normal strings. Our fixed effects are "SampleSite + TimepointName + Reads".
More information is in the Fixed effects page.
Random effects are specified as strings within brackets that includes a pipe symbol ((|)) Our random effect is `“(1|SubjectID)”.
More information is in the Random effects page



To the left of the ~ operator are the responses to be used. We do not have any responses and you are extremely unlikely to have any in maaslin3 analyses. Due to this we will not cover them any further.

title: Continuous linear plots
text:
Scatter plots with a line of best fit (regression line) are used to visualise abundance/linear modelling associations with continuous fixed effects. We will use the results from our Reads associations to interpret the metrics and plots. Preferably we’ll have no associations but as always biology has other ideas.
In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only abundance model and Reads rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display scatter plots



Create a significant result tibble only containing Reads associations with the abundance model.

#Tibble of significant abundance associations for Reads
sig_res_reads_abund_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata SampleSite rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "Reads") |>
    #Filter to retain abundance model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "abundance")
#Glimpse the tibble
sig_res_reads_abund_tbl |> dplyr::glimpse()

Extract the top 5 associations by their q-values (qval_indiviudal).

#Top 5 significant abundance read associations by qval
sig_res_reads_abund_tbl |> 
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_indivudal values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,11,12))

You’ll see the top association is:
feature: Pseudomonas_E lactis coefficient: -3.099056



In the results you will notice the fifth association (Xanthomonas campestris) has an individual q-value of >0.1 (our significance theshold). This is because its joint q-value is <0.1 so it is included in the significant results table. However, a plot of this association will not be created since its q-value is large than the max significance value we chose when running maaslin3() and the replot function.



For continuous metadata the coefficient represents the amount of change the variable causes, i.e. the slope. For example, a high positive value would represent a steep positive slope in a linear model. If we had this for our reads metadata that would indicate the abundance of the feature is likely to be higher if the sample has a higher read count compared to the samples with lower read counts. As maaslin3() analysed relative abundance values (normalization = 'TSS') we would ideally like no features to have significant associations with Reads.



Below is the plot for the association of Psedomonas_E lactis for Reads. In it we can see the slope quite steep and goes downwards. This matches our coefficient values of -3.1.




Below is the plot for the association of Lactococcus cremoris for Reads. Its slope is positive and a lot more gentle, matching our coefficient of +1.3.

title: Continuous linear plots
text: In the results you will notice the fifth association (Xanthomonas campestris) has an individual q-value of >0.1 (our significance theshold). This is because its joint q-value is <0.1 so it is included in the significant results table. However, a plot of this association will not be created since its q-value is large than the max significance value we chose when running maaslin3() and the replot function.

title: Continuous linear plots
text: For continuous metadata the coefficient represents the amount of change the variable causes, i.e. the slope. For example, a high positive value would represent a steep positive slope in a linear model. If we had this for our reads metadata that would indicate the abundance of the feature is likely to be higher if the sample has a higher read count compared to the samples with lower read counts. As maaslin3() analysed relative abundance values (normalization = 'TSS') we would ideally like no features to have significant associations with Reads.

title: Significant results table
text:
There are various output files in our output directory metagenome. The first we will look at is the text file significant_results.tsv. This contains all the significant features maaslin3() found.
This significnace is based on the indiviudal q-value or the joint q-value. Additionally the rows are ordered from most to least significant (i.e. ascending order based on the qval_indivudal column).


To view the significant results we need to first read in the file. In this case we’ll read it in as a tibble with readr::read_delim() and then glimpse it.

sig_res_tbl <- readr::read_delim(
    file = "./metagenome_output/significant_results.tsv", 
    delim="\t")
sig_res_tbl |> glimpse()

There are many columns/variables which are explained in the next page: Metrics.



You can view the whole table in R:

sig_res_tbl

With this object you can carry otu various commands to pull out specific data.
Optionally you can open the file (significant_results.tsv) by navigating to the metagenome directory through your jupyter file explorer. Once in the directory you can click on the file to open it.
Neither of these methods are convenient. In your own future analysis you can use excel but we’ll show you another option below.



We can create a html table with the package DT and its function DT::datatable(). In the function we specify the data.frame/tibble to convert to HTML. Additionally, we specify options = list(pageLength = 20) so the resulting html will show 20 rows at a time.
DT package homepage

#Convert table to html
sig_res_dt <- DT::datatable(sig_res_tbl, options = list(pageLength = 20))

The above code created an R object which we then need to save as an HTML file. We’ll carry this out with htmlwidgets::saveWidget(). The option selfcontained = must be set to TRUE for this to function correctly.
htmlwidgets::savewdigets() code documentation

#Save html
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(
    #Input widget/html
    widget = sig_res_dt,
    #Output html
    file = "./metagenome_output/significant_results.html",
    #Save as a single self contained HTML
    selfcontained = TRUE)




With the HTML created navigate into the metagenome directory via the jupyter file browser. CLick on your HTML file (significant_results.html) to open it.
The html is interactive with the following features:

Order the table by a specific column by clicking on its column names

Click on it once for descending order
Click on it twice for ascending order

Set the number of rows to display with the drop down on the top left (“Show X entries”)

Choices are: 10, 20, 25, 50, and 100

Search for specific text/numbers with the search bar on the top right

Useful if you are interested in the results of a specific species or genus

Navigate across the pages with the arrows and page numbers on the bottom right
View the total number of rows (i.e. significant features) on the bottom left

title: Summary plot
text:
We have looked at the four different types of plots and their coefficients. With this knowledge we can now look at the summary plot.
Normally, this would be the first plot you look at but it was best to look through the other plots to learn bit by bit rather than all at once.


It is good to display the summary plot in your jupyter notebook since it is a very useful overview. Type and run the below code to do so.

#View summary image
IRdisplay::display_png(file="./metagenome_output/figures/summary_plot.png")

The plot should be the same as below:



 The plot show us the top 25 features by significant associations with 2 main types of plots:

Coefficient plot

On the left side
One plot each for the most significant fixed effect comparisons
In this case it is the comparisons of Scalp against Axilla and Cheek against Axilla

Coefficient heatmap

On the right side
One plot is for the prevalence logistic modelling of all the fixed effect comparisons not in the coefficient plot
One plot is for the abundance linear modelling of all the fixed effect comparisons not in the coefficient plot


There is a legend on the right side. We’ll explain the plots and their values starting with the coefficient plot.



The coefficient plots displays information on the abundance and prevalence models. Lets go through its features/aesthetics one by one.


The features outside of the plotting area are:

x-axis: The coefficient value of the association
y-axis: The feature (e.g. Cutibacterium acnes)
Facet header: The Fixed effect comparison (e.g. SampleSite Scalp indicates the Scalp against Axilla comparison)




The features inside the plotting area can be split by Abundance and Prevalence modelling.
Lets look at the abundance modelling first.

Circles: The abundance coefficient

x-axis: The position represents its coefficient value
colour: Represents its q-value (PFDR)

Gradient goes from white to dark purple
Gradient range is displayed on the legend (1 -> 1e-31)


Horizontal line: Represents the null hypotheis

In this case this is the median coefficient value of the metadata
We set the abundance median comparison to TRUE, more info: Median comparisons






Triangle: The prevelance coefficient

x-axis: The position represents its coefficient value
colour: Represents its q-value (PFDR)

Gradient goes from white to dark cyan
Gradient range is displayed on the legend (1 -> 1e-31)


Horizontal dashed line: Represents the null hypotheis

In this case this is 0
We set the abundance median comparison to FALSE, more info: Median comparisons






There are two heatmaps, one for abundance and one for prevalence. These heatmaps are further split by metadata/effects, in this case Reads and TimepointNamePool_visits7,8,9.
The features of the plot are:

Colour: Coefficient value

The colour goes from blue (-Infinity) to white (0) to red (+Infinity).

Stars: The significance threshold

One star (*) represents a q-value less than our threshold (<0.1)
Two stars (**) represenst a q-value 10 times less than our threshold (<0.01)
Note: We have no 2 star associations in our heatmap





With the plot we’ll interpret the results of some features. After that you’ll interpret some feature associations with the guidance of MCQs.


Cutibacterium acnes is the top feature in the plot so lets discuss what the plot can tell us.
The top associations are the abundance associations for cheek and scalp. We can see both their coefficients are higher than the median coefficient via the circles in the coefficient plot. Additionally, these circle are also dark purple indicating high significance (q-value < 0.1).
Interstingly the q-value of the prevalence models are high and not significant (>0.1). This seems strange due to the fact the coefficient values are the largest in the coefficient plot (>10).
To find out why this is we can look in the significant_results.html and search for Cutibacterium acnes. You’ll see four rows, 2 for the cheek and scalp abundance modelling and 2 for their prevalence modelling. The individual q-values for the prevalence modelling are both 0? What’s causing this lack of significance?
In this case it is lack of power. The column N_not_zero shows 170 out of the 172 samples have the feature present. With only 2 samples not containing the feature the prevalence association test does not have enough power to give a significant result.
Note: In your own analysis you may need to look at all the results all_results.tsv to find feature associations that are not present in the significnat results output.



Some overall patterns to notice are:

Scalp versus axilla have similar results to cheek versus axilla, axilla was a good reference to choose
The top five features have strong positve coefficients for abuydnance for scalp and cheek
The middle features (G.adiacens to L.mirabilis, except S.capitis) have higher prevalence in the cheek samples compared to axilla
The bottom features (A.octavius to C.pseudokroppenstedtii) are in lower abundance in the scalp and cheek samples compared to the axilla samples
Features where prevalence is more likely with higher read depths also seem to have associations of lower prevalence in the TimepointNamePool_visit7,8,9

title: Categorical logistic plots
text:
Grid plots are used to visualise prevalence/logistic modelling associations with categorical effects. These show the number of samples which the feature is present and absent in for each group.
We will use the SampleSite grouping to look at categorical logistic results.
In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only prevalence model and sample site rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display scatter plots

The concept of log-odds is very important in this and the next page. It was covered earlier in this website: logistic modelling


Create a significant result tibble only containing SampleSite associations with the prevalence model.

#Create a tbl with our sig results of interest
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata SampleSIte rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "SampleSite") |>
    #Filter to retain prevalence model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "prevalence")

Extract the top 5 associations by their q-values (qval_indiviudal).

#Top 5 significnat associations for prevalence modelling of SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_indivudal values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12))

In this table all the associations have very low q-values (that is good) and reasonably high positive coefficeints.



The coefficient indicates the difference of log-odds/logit of the feature being present in query samples compared to the reference samples.
The feature in the 1st row is:

feature: Streptococcus infantis_H
name: SampleSiteCheek
coefficient: 3.230279

This indicates that the log-odds/logit of Streptococcus infantis_H being present in Cheek samples compared to Axilla samples is higher by 3.23. In other words the probability of Streptococcus infantis_H being present is higher in Cheek compared to Axilla. This could work if the log-odds of Cheek and Axilla were both negative, both positive, or if Cheek’s log-odds is positive whilst Axilla’s is negative. To know this we need to look at the plot.



Logistic categorical plots shows us how many samples the feature is present and absent in for the different groupings. We’ll look at 2 examples.


Below is the tile plots created for the logistic modeling of Streptococcus infantis_H for the sample site groupings.




In this case the plot shows:

Streptococcus infantis_H is present in 49 out of the 60 cheek samples (82%)

The feature is more likely than not (>50% chance) to be present in cheek samples

Streptococcus infantis_H is present in 7 out of the 52 axilla samples (14%)

The feature is not more likely than not (<50% chance) to be present in axilla samples


The coefficient is positive (3.2) showing the log-odds of the feature being present in cheek samples is higher than the log-odds of the feature being present in axilla samples. This would be an instance of a positive log-odds for cheek (probability > 0.5) and a negative one (probability < 0.5) for Axilla.



Lets look at a different example. Below is the tile plots for Abiotrophia sp001815865, which we’ll refer to as Abiotrophia from now on.




The cheek against axilla association of this feature has a higher positive coefficient value (3.5) than then S.infantis cheek association above (3.2). However, we can see that the log-odds for all three groupings (axill, cheek, and scalp) are negative (probabilty less than 50%).
In this case even though the log-odds is negative for cheek it is still much closer to 1 (100% probability) compared to the axilla sample. This is a reminder that the coefficient is the differenc eof the log-odds, not the log-odds value of the query group.




As a final example look at the strongest association (by q-value) with negative coefficient values. These associations indicate the feature is less likely to be present in query samples compared to reference samples. The further the value is from 0 the stronger the association.

#Top 5 significant associations with negative coefficients
# for prevalence modelling of SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_indivudal values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12))

Intersting, we now have some error messages. In this case they state “Prevalence association possibly induced by stronger abundance association”.


Below is the logistic tile plot for Cutibacterium avidum which has the error. From the plot we can see that the percentage of cheek samples with presence of the feature is much lower than for axilla samples (18% vs 52%).




To discover the meaning of the error it is best to view the abudnance/linear modelling plot for the feature. In the below plot we can see a strong association for the feature when comparing scalp to axilla. Additionally, the abundance of the feature in the scalp samples it is found in are very low. The values are all less than -10 (>0.001 relative abundance) with a median of around -16 (>0.0001 relative abundance).




Additionally, lets look at the results of all the Cutibacterium avidum associations.

#All results of Cutibacterium avidum
sig_res_tbl |>
    #Filter to only retain Cutibacterium avidum associations
    dplyr::filter(feature=="Cutibacterium avidum") |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,8,12))

Here we can see the abudnbance modeling produces much stronger significance (lower q-values) and have large negative coefficient values. Therefore, MaAsLin3 is unsure if the prevalence association is true biology or if true presence was undected in samples. A low abundance feature may not be detected due to the inherent nature of upstream wet lab procedures and/or bioinformatics processes.




Attempt the below MCQs based on the prevalence modelling with the SampleSite fixed effect:

Looking at the five most significant associations with negative coefficients, which feature did not have an error?

 Amaricoccus sp. hMGS.04904 Cutibacterium avidum Veillonella parvula_A



Looking at the five most significant associations with positive coefficients, which feature had the highest coefficient?

 Amaricoccus sp. hMGS.04904 Cutibacterium avidum Veillonella parvula_A


Looking at specific tile plots will allow you answer the next 2 questions.

What percentage of cheek samples is Gemella morbillorum present in?

 6% 42% 70%



What percentage of axilla samples is Dialister invisus present in?

 6% 42% 70%


Creating some filtering code will help answer the below questions.
Tip: The below filter function will retain rows where error is NA. Normally filtering won’t work with NA values.

#Retain rows where error is NA
#needs to be part piped code
dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na))


How many significant (based on qval_individual <0.1) scalp associations are there with no errors?

 3 143 215



How many significant (based on qval_individual <0.1) cheek associations are there with no errors?

 6% 143 215




Code solution


#MCQs
##Significant scalp associations with no errors
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Filter by q value
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |> 
    #Filter to retain Scalp associations
    dplyr::filter(name=="SampleSiteScalp") |>
    #Filter to retain rows where error is NA
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    #Count rows
    nrow()
##Significant cheek associations with no errors
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Filter by q value
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |> 
    #Filter to retain Scalp associations
    dplyr::filter(name=="SampleSiteCheek") |>
    #Filter to retain rows where error is NA
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    #Count rows
    nrow()

Below is the logistic tile plot for Cutibacterium avidum which has the error. From the plot we can see that the percentage of cheek samples with presence of the feature is much lower than for axilla samples (18% vs 52%).




To discover the meaning of the error it is best to view the abudnance/linear modelling plot for the feature. In the below plot we can see a strong association for the feature when comparing scalp to axilla. Additionally, the abundance of the feature in the scalp samples it is found in are very low. The values are all less than -10 (>0.001 relative abundance) with a median of around -16 (>0.0001 relative abundance).




Additionally, lets look at the results of all the Cutibacterium avidum associations.

#All results of Cutibacterium avidum
sig_res_tbl |>
    #Filter to only retain Cutibacterium avidum associations
    dplyr::filter(feature=="Cutibacterium avidum") |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,8,12))

Here we can see the abudnbance modeling produces much stronger significance (lower q-values) and have large negative coefficient values. Therefore, MaAsLin3 is unsure if the prevalence association is true biology or if true presence was undected in samples. A low abundance feature may not be detected due to the inherent nature of upstream wet lab procedures and/or bioinformatics processes.

title: Categorical logistic plots
text:
Attempt the below MCQs based on the prevalence modelling with the SampleSite fixed effect:

Looking at the five most significant associations with negative coefficients, which feature did not have an error?

 Amaricoccus sp. hMGS.04904 Cutibacterium avidum Veillonella parvula_A



Looking at the five most significant associations with positive coefficients, which feature had the highest coefficient?

 Amaricoccus sp. hMGS.04904 Cutibacterium avidum Veillonella parvula_A


Looking at specific tile plots will allow you answer the next 2 questions.

What percentage of cheek samples is Gemella morbillorum present in?

 6% 42% 70%



What percentage of axilla samples is Dialister invisus present in?

 6% 42% 70%


Creating some filtering code will help answer the below questions.
Tip: The below filter function will retain rows where error is NA. Normally filtering won’t work with NA values.

#Retain rows where error is NA
#needs to be part piped code
dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na))


How many significant (based on qval_individual <0.1) scalp associations are there with no errors?

 3 143 215



How many significant (based on qval_individual <0.1) cheek associations are there with no errors?

 6% 143 215




Code solution


#MCQs
##Significant scalp associations with no errors
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Filter by q value
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |> 
    #Filter to retain Scalp associations
    dplyr::filter(name=="SampleSiteScalp") |>
    #Filter to retain rows where error is NA
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    #Count rows
    nrow()
##Significant cheek associations with no errors
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Filter by q value
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |> 
    #Filter to retain Scalp associations
    dplyr::filter(name=="SampleSiteCheek") |>
    #Filter to retain rows where error is NA
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    #Count rows
    nrow()

title: MaAsLin3
text:
For our analysis we will immeidately run the maaslin3() function. It takes quite a while to run so whilst it is running read the information about the parameters and the pages on:

lme4 spcification: The formula containing the fixed and random effects
Fixed effects: The effects your hypothesis are interested in
Random effects: Effects your hypothesis is not necessarily interested in but you want to control for
Median comparison: When to and whn notto use median comparison in association testing



Run the MaAsLin3 analysis.

#Set a random seed
#We'll use 1 here but I would recommend a random number between 100 and 1000
set.seed(1)
#MaAsLin3
fit_out <- maaslin3(input_data = taxa_table,
                    input_metadata = metadata,
                    output = 'metagenome_output',
                    formula = '~ SampleSite + TimepointName + Reads + (1|SubjectID)',
                    normalization = 'TSS',
                    transform = 'LOG',
                    augment = TRUE,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    max_significance = 0.1,
                    median_comparison_abundance = TRUE,
                    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
                    small_random_effects=FALSE,
                    max_pngs = 250,
                    cores = 1)
#Set random seed back to null
set.seed(NULL)




The parameters we have used are described below.

input_data: Our formatted count table containing abundance values of species against samples.
input_metadata: Our formatted metadata table for our samples
output: Name of output directory

This will be created by the function

formula: The comparison formula in lme4 specification

Details in lme4 formula page

normalization: Normalisation of the counts before transformation and analysis. Options include:

'TTS' (default): Total Sum Scaling

Converts the counts to fractional relative abundance values so total count of each sample = 1

'CLR': Centred Log Ratio
'NONE': No normalisation

transform: Transformation of feature counts after normalisation but before analysis

Allows MaAsLin3 and us to see the log difference of features between comparisons/groups
Options include:

LOG: Base 2 log transformation (recommended)

Our TSS normalised values will all become -ve values or 0 (log(1, base=2) = 0, log(0.1, base=2)=-3.321928)

PLOG: Psuedo-log with a pseudo-count of half the dataset’s minimum non-zero abundance replacing zeroes

Suggested for metabolomics data

NONE


augment: Default is TRUE

Avoids linear separability and is almost always recommended

standardise: When TRUE it applies z-scores to continuous metadata variables so they are on the same scale

The default is TRUE and is recommended
Allows comparison of coefficients across metadata variables

max_significance: FDR corrected q-value threshold for significance

This will determine what associations are written as signifiant and what plots are created
Associations with an indivudal or joint q-value less than the threshold will be included in the significant results out
Only associations with an individual q-value lower than the threshold will be plotted

median_comparison_abundance & median_comparison_prevalence: Whether to compare coefficients against median coefficient (TRUE) or against 0

For normal taxa abundance data set median_comparison_abundance = TRUE and median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE
More info, including when to use TRUE and FALSE, in Median comparison page

small_random_effect: Replace random effects with fixed effects in logistic prevalence model

default FALSE
Use TRUE to handle low number of observations per group
More information in the Random effect page


Full information here: MaAslin3 analysis options

title: Count table
text:
The count table for MaAsLin3 needs to be a data.frame with:

Columns names of features

In our case species

Row names of sample names
Values of counts

In our case abundance values


Note: As it requires row names it cannot be a tibble.


First we will import our count data.

#Count data
#Read in count data
taxa_table <- read.csv("Metagenome_counts.csv", sep = ",", row.names=1)
#Check
str(taxa_table)
taxa_table[1:5,1:10]




Our data.frame is currently not in the correct format so we will need to use some base R and dplyr() functions to manipulate it. This includes:

Removing unwanted columns

The only taxa information we want is the species name

Transposing the data.frame
Replace NAs with 0 as MaAsLin3 won’t work with NAs


#Format count table for maaslin3
#Columns to remove
vars <- c("Domain","Kingdom","Phylum","Class","Order","Family",
            "Genus","Species","Taxonomy.Lineage")
#Edits
taxa_table <- taxa_table |>
    #Remove designated columns
    dplyr::select(-any_of(vars)) |>
    #Transpose and convert to back to data.frame
    t() |> as.data.frame() |>
    #Ensure all values are numeric
    dplyr::mutate_all(as.numeric)
#Replace NAs with 0
taxa_table[is.na(taxa_table)] <- 0
#Check
taxa_table[1:5,]

Perfect! Now its on to the metadata.

title: Metrics
text:
There are various metrics in output text files of maaslin3() for each row. Each row is a single association based on a combination of a single feature, the specific metadata comparison, and the model used (abundance or prevalence).


There is a lot of information in this plot as can be seen in the number of metrics below. Rather than interrogate the file you can read through the list and then we’ll carry onto the plots.
Although the metrics are important they do not give a full picture of the results. Rather it is better to use the metrics and plots together.



Both the files all_results.tsv and significant_results.tsv have the following columns/metrics:

feature: Feature name

Features are species in our data

metadata: Metadata name (e.g. SampleSite or Reads)
value: Metadata value

Categorical: The query group name (e.g. Cheek)
Continuous: The metadata name (e.g. Reads)

name: Variable name from model

Categorical: The metadata name and query group name combined (e.g. SampleSiteCheek)
Continuous: The metadata name (e.g. Reads)

coef: The fit coefficient

Abundance models: A one-unit change in metadata variable corresponds to 2coef fold change in the relative abundance of the feature
Prevalence models: A one-unit change in the metadatum variable corresponds to a coef change in the log-odds/logit of a feature being present
More info in the next page: Coefficients

null_hypothesis: The value of the null hypothesis against which the coefficient is tested

This value is zero when median comparison is off
This value is the median value of the feature when median comparison is on
In our results abundance model rows are median and prevalence model rows are 0

pval_individual: The p-value of the individual association
qval_individual: The false discovery rate (FDR) corrected q-value of the individual association

FDR correction is performed over all p-values without errors in the abundance and prevalence modeling together

pval_joint: p-value of the joint prevalence and abundance association

Calculated from plugging in the minimum of the association’s abundance and prevalence p-values into the Beta(1,2) CDF
Interpreted as the probability that either the abundance or prevalence association would be as extreme as observed if there was neither an abundance nor prevalence association between the feature and metadatum

qval_joint: q-value of the joint prevalence and abundance association
error: Lists any errors from the model fitting
model: Specifies whether the association is abundance or prevalence
N: Total number of data points for the feature
N_not_zero: Total number of non-zero data points for the feature

title: Median comparisons
text:
Median comparisons
When median_comparison_abundance is on
- Coefficients for a metadatum will be tested against the median coefficients for that metadatum
- Designed to account for compositionality
- The idea is that testing against zero on the relative scale in not equivalent to testing against zero in the absolute scale
- Should be used when making inference on the absolute scale when using relative abundance data
- Only p-values and q-values change
Returned coefficients are still relative abundance coefficients unless `subtract_median = TRUE` is set
When media_comparison_abundance is off
- Coefficients for a metadatum will be tested against 0
- Should be used when:
    - Testing for significant relative associations
    - Testing for absolute abundance associations under the assumption that the total absolute abundance is not changing
    - Testing for significant absolute association when supplying spike-in or total abundance with `unscaled_abundance`
